title: Constructors and invariants

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Classes and Objects, Inheritance, Records, and Object methods learning paths; atomic topic: Constructors and invariants
sources.claim: A constructor establishes a valid new object by initializing required state, validating arguments, and delegating to another constructor or superclass before ordinary instance use.

sources.locator: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21; Java SE 21; JLS §§8.1-8.10 and 15.21.3: classes, constructors, records, and reference equality; atomic topic: Constructors and invariants
sources.claim: A constructor establishes a valid new object by initializing required state, validating arguments, and delegating to another constructor or superclass before ordinary instance use.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A constructor establishes a valid new object by initializing required state, validating arguments, and delegating to another constructor or superclass before ordinary instance use.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A two-argument constructor validates normalized bounds, while a convenience constructor delegates with this(...) so the invariant has one implementation. Your goal is to implement constructors that prevent invalid observable object state. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Method contracts and parameters; pf data records; Classes, fields and methods. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A constructor establishes a valid new object by initializing required state, validating arguments, and delegating to another constructor or superclass before ordinary instance use. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Constructors are not inherited or dynamically dispatched, this(...) or super(...) ordering is constrained, partially initialized this can escape, and records have their own constructor forms. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Java supplies a no-argument constructor even after any constructor is declared, and validation can be postponed safely until the first method call.

includes: Initialize all required state, validate arguments and chain constructors deliberately.
Implement constructors that prevent invalid observable object state.
excludes: Constructors are not inherited or dynamically dispatched, this(...) or super(...) ordering is constrained, partially initialized this can escape, and records have their own constructor forms.

outcomes: Implement constructors that prevent invalid observable object state.

examples.title: Constructors and invariants: direct language case
examples.situation: A two-argument constructor validates normalized bounds, while a convenience constructor delegates with this(...) so the invariant has one implementation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A two-argument constructor validates normalized bounds, while a convenience constructor delegates with this(...) so the invariant has one implementation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Declare every required construction path explicitly and establish the complete invariant before the new reference becomes observable.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Declare every required construction path explicitly and establish the complete invariant before the new reference becomes observable.

examples.title: Constructors and invariants: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A constructor rejects null before assigning the field and never registers this with another component until all invariant checks have passed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A constructor rejects null before assigning the field and never registers this with another component until all invariant checks have passed.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Constructors are not inherited or dynamically dispatched, this(...) or super(...) ordering is constrained, partially initialized this can escape, and records have their own constructor forms.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Java supplies a no-argument constructor even after any constructor is declared, and validation can be postponed safely until the first method call.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement constructors that prevent invalid observable object state.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Java supplies a no-argument constructor even after any constructor is declared, and validation can be postponed safely until the first method call.
misconceptions.correction: Declare every required construction path explicitly and establish the complete invariant before the new reference becomes observable.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Java supplies a no-argument constructor even after any constructor is declared, and validation can be postponed safely until the first method call.

analogy.example: a secured service object whose constructor admits only valid state and whose public desk exposes approved behavior can introduce Constructors and invariants, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: understanding encapsulation, construction, identity, equality, and records
limitations: object references, hashing, shallow immutability, and garbage collection are not physical ownership rules

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement constructors that prevent invalid observable object state. Keep this boundary in view: Constructors are not inherited or dynamically dispatched, this(...) or super(...) ordering is constrained, partially initialized this can escape, and records have their own constructor forms.

Why is this belief incorrect: Java supplies a no-argument constructor even after any constructor is declared, and validation can be postponed safely until the first method call.
Predict this case from memory: A constructor rejects null before assigning the field and never registers this with another component until all invariant checks have passed.
